title: Scientists Make Solar Cells Work Better Than Ever
summary: Solar cells are devices that use sunlight to make electricity, helping us fight climate change by reducing our need for fossil fuels like coal and oil. Normally, solar cells can only use about one-third of the sunlight that hits them because some light has too little energy and some loses extra energy as heat. Scientists from Japan and Germany have found a new way to make solar cells work much better. They used a special material called a molybdenum-based metal complex to capture extra energy from a process called singlet fission. This process can create two energy particles from one, allowing the solar cells to achieve over 130% efficiency, which is higher than the usual 100% limit. This breakthrough could lead to more powerful solar panels in the future, making clean energy more effective and affordable.

background_read: Solar power is a clean energy source that comes from the sun. Solar cells, also called photovoltaic cells, are used in solar panels to convert sunlight into electricity. They help reduce pollution and fight climate change by replacing fossil fuels. However, traditional solar cells have limits because they can't use all the sunlight efficiently. Scientists are always looking for ways to improve them, like using new materials or processes to capture more energy. This article talks about a recent discovery that makes solar cells work better than ever before.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Kyushu University in Japan and Johannes Gutenberg University in Germany
WHAT: Developed a new solar cell using a molybdenum-based metal complex to achieve over 130% efficiency
WHERE: Research was conducted in Japan and Germany
WHY: To overcome the limits of solar cells and make clean energy more effective

perspectives.description: Scientists are finding new ways to improve solar technology to help the environment.
